findings.title: POST /v1/brands — workspace_id from request body, not JWT; session-workspace binding missing
findings.detail: organizationId is set from parsed.data.workspace_id (request body), not from auth.workspaceId (JWT). A user who holds owner/brand_admin membership in both org-A and org-B can send a POST /v1/brands with workspace_id=org-B while their session JWT binds them to org-A. The membership check in BrandService.create (brand.service.ts:68) uses the body-derived organizationId as the lookup key, so it correctly gates membership — but it grants membership in the body-specified org regardless of which org the session is currently scoped to. This violates the MA-02 workspace-binding principle: the session workspace context (auth.workspaceId) must govern which workspace a mutation targets. Fix: replace parsed.data.workspace_id with auth.workspaceId at brand.routes.ts:43 and validate body.workspace_id === auth.workspaceId (or remove it from the body schema entirely). Additionally, auth.role (JWT claim, line 47) is passed to BrandService.create as requestingRole but the service ignores this value and re-derives role from the DB membership row (brand.service.ts:69) — so role enforcement is sound, but the unused parameter is a confusion hazard.

findings.title: brand_self_read PERMISSIVE OR — brand-A row readable in brand-B session via switcher list (intentional design, undocumented in policy header)
findings.detail: brand_self_read is PERMISSIVE and ORs with brand_isolation (0004, PERMISSIVE FOR ALL). A brand-B session (GUC=B) can read brand-A rows from the `brand` table because brand_self_read's membership subquery returns all member-brands in the active org. This is the correct intended behavior for the switcher list (documented in the migration header and architecture plan). However, the pg_policies `qual` for brand_self_read does not explicitly state that cross-brand reads on the `brand` table are intentional — a future developer auditing the policies might incorrectly conclude this is a leakage path. The isolation-fuzz AC-7 test at pg.test.ts:564 acknowledges this (expect(brandRowCount).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(0)) but does not assert a specific value — a positive assertion that brand-A IS visible to a brand-B session on the `brand` table would make the intent explicit and prevent a false-positive finding in future reviews. This is a documentation/test-clarity finding; the isolation itself is correct for `connector_instance` and all other brand-scoped tables (proven by AC-7 fuzz run: 0 rows).

findings.title: create-brand-dialog passes workspace_id from TanStack Query cache, not from the session JWT; cache-miss sends empty string
findings.detail: getActiveWorkspaceId(queryClient) reads the first workspace from the ['workspace', 'list'] cache and can return null on first load or in any fresh tab. When null, line 143 falls back to '' (empty string), which is sent as workspace_id to POST /v1/brands. The backend will then call findByUserAndOrg(userId, '', null, ctx) which will return null (no membership for empty-string workspace) → 403 FORBIDDEN with a confusing error. The correct fix is to source workspace_id from the session JWT (auth.workspaceId from useCurrentUser/bff-me) — the same source used by the set-brand route — rather than from the workspace list cache. This also aligns with the dependency the backend should have (SEC-MB-1 fix will enforce this). Low exploit risk (it just 403s) but creates a broken UX and confusing error path.

findings.title: audit.append order vs mintSessionToken: audit row written AFTER mint; inconsistency with comment claim
findings.detail: The code comment at line 693 states 'Audit (MA-09) — written after a successful membership+archived check', and it IS written after the mint (mintSessionToken is called at line 690, audit.append at line 696). The architecture plan §A2.7 states 'Audit written after successful membership+archived check' which is ambiguous about whether it comes before or after mintSessionToken. If mintSessionToken throws (e.g. JWTSignError) after audit.append, the audit entry records a brand.switch that the user's cookie will NOT reflect (the switch effectively did not happen from the user perspective). The plan acknowledges this as acceptable (matches existing pattern). Noted as LOW: it is a known trade-off, not a security defect, but the audit row will mislead forensics in the rare throw-after-audit case.

findings.title: Pre-existing unit test failure: critical-paths.test.ts:143 expects expiresIn=900 but ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRY_SECS=3600
findings.detail: Backend developer confirms this is pre-existing since fix/token-1h-expiry-logout on the base branch (git stash proof). Not introduced by feat-multi-brand. Does not affect isolation or brand-switch security. Noted for QA remediation.
